deductions.evidence_turns: Transcript 1, Turn 10; Transcript 2, Turn 10; Transcript 3, Turn 10
deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students arrive at improved understanding after earlier confusion, the tutor moves forward with next steps rather than prompting explicit reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the new approach worked. For example, in T1 Turn 10 the tutor asks for a draft without pausing to note the student's progression; in T2 Turn 10 the tutor asks for a summary sentence without reflecting on the student's journey; in T3 Turn 10 the tutor asks for a position without noting what the student figured out.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts the tutor consistently applies Socratic method, refuses to walk through cases for the student, and guides discovery through targeted questions. Key ideas (the act consequentialist rule, means vs. side effect, the criticism of the theory) are all student-generated with the tutor probing and confirming rather than supplying. Scaffolding is well-calibrated to each student's level, and the tutor builds on correct student contributions rather than restarting. The one consistent gap is the absence of explicit process reflection after students improve: the tutor moves to the next task without pausing to ask what changed in the student's thinking or why the new approach worked, triggering a 1.3.C.a deduction. No other rubric criteria are clearly violated.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides near-submission-ready output, never rewrites student text, and never states the key conclusion before the student derives it. All three transcripts show the student generating the core claims (the consequentialist rule, the means/side-effect distinction, the criticism) through guided questioning. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledges correct student steps and builds on them; in Transcript 1 the tutor even explicitly validates partial answers before pushing further. No evidence of ignoring correct progress or skipping diagnostic questioning. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor provides implicit rationale for its approach (e.g., T1 Turn 2: 'That would do the thinking for you') satisfying 1.3.A; it gives specific process-level feedback (e.g., 'don't flatten the cases too fast,' 'focus on structure, not the label') satisfying 1.3.B. However, in all three transcripts, when students reach improved understanding after earlier confusion, the tutor immediately pivots to the next task without prompting reflection on what changed—triggering 1.3.C.a for 2 points. 2.1–2.2: No repetitive spiraling, no off-topic drift, no misunderstood assignment framing left uncorrected. 3.1–3.2: Responses are concise and interactive; tone is consistently supportive and formative.
